[2018-02-28 09:26] jamesa7171: to add on to the ask-a-dev conversation, it seems to me that the needs of new/low elo players are much more important when discussing casual mode than the needs of high elo players
[2018-02-28 09:26] jamesa7171: so if e.g. 90 second casual match would be good for new players but bad for high elo players, tough for the high elo players (imo)
[2018-02-28 09:27] crash_overlord: I don't know of any "high elo players" in other games that play a "casual" mode
[2018-02-28 09:27] crash_overlord: So yes, design it around new players
[2018-02-28 09:28] crash_overlord: But I do believe 60 seconds should be the highest time setting to introduce people to the game
[2018-02-28 09:28] crash_overlord: Too much time often leads to more confusion
[2018-02-28 09:29] Deleted User (Deleted User): i use casual match a ton when i don't want to risk rating
[2018-02-28 09:30] kristian1103 (Arkanishu): because rating is so important :LoganHaa:
[2018-02-28 09:32] p0lari (Polari): I beat up casual bots when I feel like durdling rather than making serious effort
[2018-02-28 09:32] Deleted User (Deleted User): I think stalling hurts noobs more than pros
[2018-02-28 09:33] p0lari (Polari): every now and then I try to get a human casual match then get back to bots when it doesn't happen
[2018-02-28 09:33] Deleted User (Deleted User): I can just res and not care. Or avoid it altogether
[2018-02-28 09:33] p0lari (Polari): would never queue vs humans if it was 90s and very rarely if 60s
[2018-02-28 09:34] p0lari (Polari): it's fine as long as neither player is actually using that time, but then you run into someone who actually wants to do that and just want to kill yourself

--- Thread (50 messages, 2018-02-28) ---
[2018-02-28 09:58] p0lari (Polari): I can't help but wonder how much of the arguing in favour of 90s is because it's already an option for ranked and going by "higher is better", and how much because of thinking it's actually a sweet spot
[2018-02-28 09:59] p0lari (Polari): like if there was a 120s setting would you still say casual should have that because bigger is better for newer players? what about 180s?
[2018-02-28 09:59] p0lari (Polari): personally I had all settings on for ranked until the first time I got an opponent who used all of that, and then turned off 60s and 90s forever
[2018-02-28 10:00] p0lari (Polari): I find 45s to be around the limit of what I can wait for a turn before losing interest in the game
[2018-02-28 10:00] mtanzer: I still feel rushed on 45s in base8-11 a lot
[2018-02-28 10:00] punf: i find 10s to be around the limit of what i can wait for a turn before losing interest in the game
[2018-02-28 10:00] mtanzer: people are different
[2018-02-28 10:00] mabish: How about devs just get feedback from actual new players instead of arguing about what makes the most sense
[2018-02-28 10:00] punf: hrm thats a lame answer but i cant help but support it
[2018-02-28 10:01] p0lari (Polari): sure, I can run out of time too on 45s in complex high econ mid to lategame turns
[2018-02-28 10:01] mabish: I totally feel I need 90s timers in my first games I take so much more time counting units and counting tech needs
[2018-02-28 10:01] mtanzer: honestly on 60s sometimes I'll use the full 60 t1 and then an additional large amount on t2 just trying to read the set properly
[2018-02-28 10:01] punf: hestiation is always easy, rarely useful
[2018-02-28 10:02] p0lari (Polari): but unless you can trust your opponent to only use the time there or have a fancier timebank implemented, I'm going to assume my opponent is using that time every turn for the purposes of deciding which time controls I'm ok with
[2018-02-28 10:02] p0lari (Polari): and I'm not ok sitting through a game where my opponent contemplates for 90s every turn through the earlygame
[2018-02-28 10:02] mtanzer: I don't choose what I'm ok with by worst case but by average case
[2018-02-28 10:02] mtanzer: like if 2% of people use the full 90s every turn that won't make me stop queueing 90s
[2018-02-28 10:03] mabish: And more importantly there are a ton of people who just want to jump in and play vs humans until they get good at the game even if playing campaign/bot games is more "optimal".
[2018-02-28 10:04] p0lari (Polari): but then if you don't want to sit through 90s turns and only play it because most people don't... why not just play 45s instead?
[2018-02-28 10:07] mtanzer: some days I don't feel like sitting through multiple 90s turns and I'll turn those off.  I mean 90s to me is exactly as the description says, "relaxed."  Even if I'm playing most of my turns in 45s it's still nice to also not feel under time pressure at all
[2018-02-28 10:07] mtanzer: and if most players are using it that way too then this works fine for me
[2018-02-28 10:07] punf: is this discussion going anywhere? i dont think this discussion is going anywhere
[2018-02-28 10:10] mtanzer: regarding casual time controls and new player experience, I would imagine it's common for new players to not know what all the units do and spend some of their time reading the tooltips
[2018-02-28 10:10] crash_overlord: The only reason I say 90s might be too long is that while I might not "know" this, my intuition is that often times being pressured leads to actively making decisions while no pressure leads to a lot of undecisivness and "feelings of overbearingness". For example, having 20s to look at the set you notice 3 defending green units, 3 big blue units and 2 small red units. Now while you have no clue whats better, you have to "decide" on one and play it out. This might lose or win, most likely lose, but you decided and now you know that decisions was correct/incorrect. 

Given 100s you'll see the same thing, then take 80 seconds thinking about which is better/worse before making a decision. While this is good when you can actually think that through, as a new players your thought process is "Ok this costs 3 blue and has 4 attack compared to the red strategy which is cheap"... You have no basis of comparison so the amount of value "thinking" about the set is minimal. Once you're past the aditional 10-20seconds of thinking that you're required to do to see the differences in the blue v red strategy, you end up spending another 50 seconds being unable to decide because there is no way you can decide, as you have no reason to be able to. You don't know what's better so you have no basis to make the decission of which is better. It leaves you with a sense of... "unknowingness" and helplessness and playing the game ends up feeling kinda shitty. 

Compared to being pressured, sure sometimes you'll lose to time control, but you'll never feel "lost" as badly as in a position of no pressure.

Atleast from what I intuitively think. I 100% admit I could be wrong. I also think this doesn't apply to all people.
[2018-02-28 10:11] punf: 80 seconds thinking about which one is better? and you think this will help you reach the correct decision?
[2018-02-28 10:11] punf: hestiation is always easy, rarely useful
[2018-02-28 10:12] crash_overlord: Illiterate punf strikes again :POGGERS:
[2018-02-28 10:12] punf: oh dear yes i will admit i read that quickly, and upon reading the rest it looks like you actually agree with me
[2018-02-28 10:13] mtanzer: I think crash brings up a good point that when players don't have much basis to make a decision they might still try to use the extra time to make it when in reality they'd be better off learning from experience by just choosing one and seeing what happens
[2018-02-28 10:14] mtanzer: but the shorter times have the downside of new players feeling more rushed and not having time to read what things do
[2018-02-28 10:14] punf: i brought up that point before it was cool
[2018-02-28 10:14] crash_overlord: Yeah, optimally you want players to both have time to read all the new units and absorb what they do while also pressuring them to making a move.
[2018-02-28 10:15] crash_overlord: But isn't that why base+5 is a thing?
[2018-02-28 10:15] mtanzer: 5 is easier but I think it's easy for us to forget how overwhelming this game can be at first

messages.content:
The only reason I say 90s might be too long is that while I might not "know" this, my intuition is that often times being pressured leads to actively making decisions while no pressure leads to a lot of undecisivness and "feelings of overbearingness". For example, having 20s to look at the set you notice 3 defending green units, 3 big blue units and 2 small red units. Now while you have no clue whats better, you have to "decide" on one and play it out. This might lose or win, most likely lose, but you decided and now you know that decisions was correct/incorrect. 

Given 100s you'll see the same thing, then take 80 seconds thinking about which is better/worse before making a decision. While this is good when you can actually think that through, as a new players your thought process is "Ok this costs 3 blue and has 4 attack compared to the red strategy which is cheap"... You have no basis of comparison so the amount of value "thinking" about the set is minimal. Once you're past the aditional 10-20seconds of thinking that you're required to do to see the differences in the blue v red strategy, you end up spending another 50 seconds being unable to decide because there is no way you can decide, as you have no reason to be able to. You don't know what's better so you have no basis to make the decission of which is better. It leaves you with a sense of... "unknowingness" and helplessness and playing the game ends up feeling kinda shitty. 

Compared to being pressured, sure sometimes you'll lose to time control, but you'll never feel "lost" as badly as in a position of no pressure.

Atleast from what I intuitively think. I 100% admit I could be wrong. I also think this doesn't apply to all people.

--- Thread (36 messages, 2018-02-28) ---
[2018-02-28 10:36] crash_overlord: Yes @Polari While 500s is a lot of time, you can actually spend all of it thinking about the game. Most of it might not be applicable to the exact game, but if you predict your opponents buildpath you can think 5+ turns deep. Now I agree with you here, 500s for an average player is probably far too much time to reasonably expect them to think for future turns. It's also probably on average not that "fun". But it's not because it's "Wasting time". It's because most people won't actively be spending that time thinking about the game and will rather just wait for their turn. Leading to a feeling of boredom and "not playing the game".

If you look at other turn based games like Civilization or XCOM a "turn" can take upwards of 10 minutes. It doesn't mean it's "wasting time". That's just the time you spend playing it. Now while this direct comparison doesn't really help anything as far as a PVP game goes, I'm ussing to illustrate the point that no arbitrary "time" is "wasting time".

messages.content:
Yes @Polari While 500s is a lot of time, you can actually spend all of it thinking about the game. Most of it might not be applicable to the exact game, but if you predict your opponents buildpath you can think 5+ turns deep. Now I agree with you here, 500s for an average player is probably far too much time to reasonably expect them to think for future turns. It's also probably on average not that "fun". But it's not because it's "Wasting time". It's because most people won't actively be spending that time thinking about the game and will rather just wait for their turn. Leading to a feeling of boredom and "not playing the game".

If you look at other turn based games like Civilization or XCOM a "turn" can take upwards of 10 minutes. It doesn't mean it's "wasting time". That's just the time you spend playing it.
